K–2.title: Picture Algorithms: Telling a Story in Order
K–2.objective: Students will create and follow simple picture-based algorithms to understand how sequence affects outcomes in stories and everyday tasks.
K–2.task: Students receive a set of mixed-up picture cards showing a simple daily routine (e.g., brushing teeth). Working in pairs, they arrange the cards into a logical sequence and then act out the steps.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher listens as students justify why one picture should come before another, prompting them to use sequencing language such as 'first,' 'next,' and 'last.'
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students create their own 3–4 step picture algorithm on blank cards and explain the sequence to a partner.
K–2.udl: Provide tactile cards, visual icons, optional verbal retell, and sentence frames. Offer simplified 3-step sets for emerging learners.
K–2.materials: Picture cards, blank index cards, crayons, story mats.
K–2.vocabulary: sequence, order, first, next, last, algorithm
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on sequencing.
2. Model arranging a simple picture algorithm.
3. Students work in pairs to order mixed-up cards.
4. Students create their own picture algorithm.
5. Share-out and reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide fewer cards for students needing reduced cognitive load. Offer challenge sets with distractor cards for advanced learners.
K–2.reflection: What was the most important step in your algorithm? What would happen if you changed the order?

K–2.title: Robot Paths: Building and Extending Patterns
K–2.objective: Students will identify, extend, and create repeating patterns to build foundational skills for algorithms and loops.
K–2.task: Students use colored tiles to build a repeating pattern that represents a robot’s movement path. They then extend the pattern and predict the robot’s next moves.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher asks students to describe the pattern rule and checks whether they can extend the pattern correctly.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students create their own repeating pattern path and explain the rule in writing or verbally.
K–2.udl: Provide color tiles, printed pattern strips, and digital pattern builders. Offer sentence frames for describing rules.
K–2.materials: Color tiles, pattern strips, robot path mats.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on repeating patterns.
2. Model building a simple pattern.
3. Students build and extend patterns.
4. Students create their own pattern path.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide pre-made patterns for students who need support. Challenge advanced learners to create ABBA or ABC patterns.
K–2.reflection: How did you know what came next in your pattern? What rule did you use?

K–2.title: Fix the Robot: Debugging Movement Commands
K–2.objective: Students will identify and correct errors in simple movement commands to understand debugging as a problem-solving process.
K–2.task: Students receive a set of robot movement cards (forward, turn left, turn right). They follow a flawed sequence on a floor grid and work together to find and fix the mistake.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher observes students as they test the sequence, prompting them to explain where the robot went wrong.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students correct the flawed sequence and draw the corrected path on a grid worksheet.
K–2.udl: Provide large floor grids, tactile arrows, and optional verbal explanations. Offer simplified paths for emerging learners.
K–2.materials: Floor grid, arrow cards, robot icons, worksheets.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on debugging.
2. Model finding an error.
3. Students test a flawed sequence.
4. Students correct the sequence.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide shorter paths for students needing support. Challenge advanced learners with two-step errors.
K–2.reflection: How did you know where the mistake was? What strategy helped you fix it?

K–2.title: Sort the Animals: Classification and Attributes
K–2.objective: Students will classify objects based on shared attributes and explain their reasoning, building early data science skills.
K–2.task: Students sort animal picture cards into categories they choose (e.g., number of legs, habitat). They refine categories after peer discussion.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher asks students to explain why two animals belong together and checks for attribute-based reasoning.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students create a labeled chart showing their final categories and present their reasoning.
K–2.udl: Provide printed cards, digital sorting tools, and sentence frames. Offer audio descriptions for accessibility.
K–2.materials: Animal cards, chart paper, markers.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on attributes.
2. Model sorting.
3. Students sort animals.
4. Peer feedback.
5. Final chart creation.
K–2.differentiation: Provide pre-made categories for support. Challenge advanced learners to create multi-level categories.
K–2.reflection: What was the hardest animal to place? Why?

K–2.title: Handwashing Algorithms: Step-by-Step Instructions
K–2.objective: Students will create and follow step-by-step algorithms for real-world tasks, understanding that precise instructions lead to predictable outcomes.
K–2.task: Students work in small groups to create a handwashing algorithm using picture cards. They test their algorithm with a partner acting it out.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher listens as students test their algorithm, prompting them to clarify vague steps.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students create a final illustrated algorithm and explain why each step is important.
K–2.udl: Provide picture cards, visual timers, and optional verbal explanations. Offer simplified steps for emerging learners.
K–2.materials: Picture cards, chart paper, crayons.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on algorithms.
2. Model a handwashing algorithm.
3. Students build and test algorithms.
4. Students create final illustrated algorithms.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide pre-made steps for support. Challenge advanced learners to add conditional steps (e.g., 'If hands are still soapy…').
K–2.reflection: What would happen if you skipped a step? How do you know your algorithm works?

K–2.title: Dance Loops: Repeating Movement Patterns
K–2.objective: Students will understand loops by creating and performing repeating movement patterns.
K–2.task: Students design a short dance sequence (e.g., clap–clap–spin) and repeat it multiple times to demonstrate a loop.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher observes whether students can identify the repeating part of their dance sequence.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students draw their dance loop using pictures or symbols and label the repeated section.
K–2.udl: Provide visual icons for movements, model a sample loop, and offer seated movement options.
K–2.materials: Movement cards, music, chart paper.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on loops.
2. Model a movement loop.
3. Students create and perform loops.
4. Students draw their loops.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Offer pre-made loops for support. Challenge advanced learners to create nested loops.
K–2.reflection: What part of your dance repeats? How do you know it’s a loop?

K–2.title: Robot Maze Navigation
K–2.objective: Students will program a floor robot to navigate a simple maze using step-by-step commands.
K–2.task: Students work in pairs to plan a path through a taped floor maze, then program a Bee‑Bot or similar robot to follow the path.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks whether students can predict the robot’s next move before running the program.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students complete the maze and draw their robot’s path on a worksheet.
K–2.udl: Provide tactile arrows, simplified mazes, and verbal rehearsal options.
K–2.materials: Floor robot, maze tape, arrow cards, path worksheets.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on robot commands.
2. Model navigating a simple maze.
3. Students plan and test paths.
4. Students record final paths.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Offer simpler mazes for support. Challenge advanced learners with obstacles or conditional choices.
K–2.reflection: What was the trickiest part of programming your robot? How did you fix mistakes?

K–2.title: Neighborhood Maps: Giving and Following Directions
K–2.objective: Students will create and follow simple directional algorithms using a neighborhood map.
K–2.task: Students use a classroom or neighborhood map to write a simple set of directions (e.g., 'Go forward 2 blocks, turn right'). Partners follow the directions to reach a destination.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher listens as students test their directions and checks for clarity and precision.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students create a final illustrated direction set and label the path on a map.
K–2.udl: Provide printed maps, tactile arrows, and optional verbal directions. Offer simplified maps for emerging learners.
K–2.materials: Maps, arrow cards, crayons.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on giving directions.
2. Model writing a simple path.
3. Students write and test directions.
4. Students create final illustrated paths.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide pre-made direction starters for support. Challenge advanced learners with branching paths.
K–2.reflection: What made your directions easy or hard to follow?

K–2.title: CT Choice Centers
K–2.objective: Students will explore computational thinking skills through a choice of hands-on centers.
K–2.task: Students rotate through centers such as pattern building, robot paths, sorting games, and sequencing puzzles. They choose which centers to complete first.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher observes students at each center, asking questions about patterns, sequences, and rules.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students complete a reflection sheet describing what they learned at two centers.
K–2.udl: Provide multiple center options, visual instructions, and flexible pacing.
K–2.materials: Center bins, pattern tiles, sorting cards, robot mats.
K–2.procedures:
1. Introduce centers.
2. Model expectations.
3. Students rotate through centers.
4. Students complete reflection sheets.
5. Share-out.
K–2.differentiation: Allow students to stay longer at centers that match their readiness. Offer challenge cards at each center.
K–2.reflection: Which center helped you learn the most? Why?

K–2.title: Design a Helper Tool: Early Prototyping
K–2.objective: Students will design and build a simple prototype that solves a classroom problem using everyday materials.
K–2.task: Students identify a small problem (e.g., 'My pencils roll off my desk') and build a prototype solution using craft materials.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher asks students to explain what problem their prototype solves and how it works.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students present their prototype and describe how it could be improved.
K–2.udl: Provide visual examples, flexible materials, and optional verbal explanations.
K–2.materials: Paper, tape, cardboard, pipe cleaners, markers.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on prototypes.
2. Brainstorm classroom problems.
3. Students build prototypes.
4. Students present and reflect.
5. Optional redesign.
K–2.differentiation: Provide problem prompts for support. Challenge advanced learners to add moving parts.
K–2.reflection: What would you change about your prototype if you had more time?

K–2.title: If It’s Raining: Everyday Conditionals
K–2.objective: Students will understand conditionals by applying if/then logic to everyday weather-related decisions.
K–2.task: Students receive weather cards (rainy, sunny, snowy) and choose matching action cards (use umbrella, wear sunglasses). They match them using if/then statements.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher asks students to verbalize their reasoning using the phrase 'If it is __, then I __.'
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students create a mini-book with three if/then weather rules and illustrate each one.
K–2.udl: Provide picture cards, sentence frames, and optional verbal explanations. Offer simplified choices for emerging learners.
K–2.materials: Weather cards, action cards, mini-book templates.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on conditionals.
2. Model matching weather to actions.
3. Students match cards.
4. Students create mini-books.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide pre-matched examples for support. Challenge advanced learners with if/then/else choices.
K–2.reflection: How did the weather change your decision? What rule did you use?

K–2.title: Class Survey: Collecting and Representing Data
K–2.objective: Students will collect simple data through a class survey and represent it using a picture graph.
K–2.task: Students ask classmates a survey question (e.g., favorite fruit) and record responses using tally marks or stickers. They then create a picture graph.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks whether students correctly record each response and can explain what the data shows.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students create a final picture graph and write or dictate one conclusion about the data.
K–2.udl: Provide tally sheets, stickers, digital graphing tools, and sentence frames.
K–2.materials: Survey sheets, stickers, chart paper.
K–2.vocabulary: data, survey, graph, tally, more, fewer
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on data.
2. Model collecting responses.
3. Students conduct surveys.
4. Students build picture graphs.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide pre-filled tally sheets for support. Challenge advanced learners to compare two categories.
K–2.reflection: What did your graph show? What surprised you?

K–2.title: Break It Down: Morning Routine Decomposition
K–2.objective: Students will break a familiar task into smaller steps to understand decomposition.
K–2.task: Students brainstorm all the steps in a morning routine (e.g., wake up, brush teeth, get dressed) and organize them into a step-by-step chart.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher asks students to explain why a big task needs to be broken into smaller steps.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students create a final illustrated step chart showing at least four decomposed steps.
K–2.udl: Provide picture icons, sentence frames, and optional verbal explanations. Offer simplified routines for emerging learners.
K–2.materials: Picture icons, chart paper, markers.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on decomposition.
2. Model breaking down a task.
3. Students brainstorm steps.
4. Students create step charts.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide pre-made step cards for support. Challenge advanced learners to add conditional steps.
K–2.reflection: Which step was most important? What would happen if you skipped it?

K–2.title: Story Coding: Turning Stories into Algorithms
K–2.objective: Students will translate a simple story into a sequence of commands, understanding that stories can be represented as algorithms.
K–2.task: Students listen to a short story and identify key actions. They then create a sequence of picture commands that retell the story.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks whether students can identify story actions and place them in order.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students create a final command sequence and present it to a partner, who retells the story using the algorithm.
K–2.udl: Provide picture cards, audio story options, and sentence frames. Offer simplified stories for emerging readers.
K–2.materials: Story cards, picture commands, audio story.
K–2.procedures:
1. Read or play a short story.
2. Model identifying actions.
3. Students create command sequences.
4. Partner retell.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide pre-selected actions for support. Challenge advanced learners to add loops or conditionals.
K–2.reflection: How did turning the story into commands help you understand it better?

K–2.title: Tangram Debugging: Fix the Shape
K–2.objective: Students will identify and correct errors in tangram arrangements, building spatial reasoning and debugging skills.
K–2.task: Students receive tangram puzzles with intentional mistakes (wrong piece, flipped piece). They work in pairs to find and fix the errors.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher observes students as they test piece placement and asks them to explain why a piece does or does not fit.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students complete a corrected tangram puzzle and draw the final shape.
K–2.udl: Provide tactile tangram pieces, digital tangram apps, and visual outlines. Offer simplified puzzles for emerging learners.
K–2.materials: Tangram sets, puzzle cards, drawing paper.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on debugging.
2. Model fixing a tangram error.
3. Students debug puzzles.
4. Students draw final shapes.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide fewer pieces for support. Challenge advanced learners with silhouette-only puzzles.
K–2.reflection: How did you know which piece was wrong? What strategy helped you fix it?

K–2.title: Safe Choices Online
K–2.objective: Students will identify safe and unsafe online behaviors and practice making responsible choices.
K–2.task: Students sort picture cards into 'Safe Online' and 'Not Safe Online' categories, then role-play safe choices.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher listens as students justify why a behavior is safe or unsafe.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students complete a simple worksheet circling safe online behaviors.
K–2.udl: Provide picture cards, verbal explanations, and sentence frames. Offer simplified choices for emerging learners.
K–2.materials: Picture cards, sorting mats, worksheets.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on online safety.
2. Model sorting behaviors.
3. Students sort cards.
4. Role-play safe choices.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide fewer cards for support. Challenge advanced learners with 'what if' scenarios.
K–2.reflection: What is one safe choice you can make online?

K–2.title: Sound Patterns: Clap, Tap, Snap
K–2.objective: Students will identify and extend repeating sound patterns to strengthen pattern recognition skills.
K–2.task: Students listen to a sequence of sounds (clap–tap–clap–tap) and identify the pattern. They then create their own sound patterns for classmates to repeat.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks whether students can correctly identify the repeating unit.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students record their own sound pattern using symbols and perform it for the class.
K–2.udl: Provide visual icons for sounds, model patterns, and offer movement alternatives.
K–2.materials: Sound cards, chart paper.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on sound patterns.
2. Model identifying a pattern.
3. Students listen and repeat.
4. Students create their own patterns.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide simple AB patterns for support. Challenge advanced learners with ABC or AAB patterns.
K–2.reflection: How did you know what sound came next?

K–2.title: Robot Buttons: Cause and Effect
K–2.objective: Students will understand cause and effect by exploring how pressing different robot buttons triggers specific actions.
K–2.task: Students experiment with a simple robot and record what each button does. They then predict outcomes before pressing buttons.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher asks students to predict what will happen before pressing a button.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students complete a cause-and-effect chart showing each button and its action.
K–2.udl: Provide tactile buttons, visual charts, and verbal modeling. Offer simplified robots for emerging learners.
K–2.materials: Robots, button charts, markers.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on cause and effect.
2. Model testing a button.
3. Students explore robot buttons.
4. Students complete charts.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide fewer buttons for support. Challenge advanced learners with multi-button predictions.
K–2.reflection: Which button surprised you? Why?

K–2.title: Arrow Coding: Build a Path
K–2.objective: Students will create simple algorithms using directional arrows to guide a character across a grid.
K–2.task: Students place arrow cards on a grid to move a character from start to finish, avoiding obstacles.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks whether students can explain why they chose each arrow.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students complete a final path and draw the route on a worksheet.
K–2.udl: Provide tactile arrows, simplified grids, and optional verbal explanations.
K–2.materials: Grid mats, arrow cards, character tokens.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on directional coding.
2. Model building a path.
3. Students create paths.
4. Students complete worksheets.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide shorter paths for support. Challenge advanced learners with obstacles or conditional arrows.
K–2.reflection: What strategy helped you choose the right arrows?

K–2.title: Sort It Out: Organizing Classroom Objects
K–2.objective: Students will classify objects based on shared attributes and explain their sorting choices.
K–2.task: Students sort classroom objects (markers, blocks, erasers) into categories they choose and label each group.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher asks students to explain why two objects belong together.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students create a labeled chart showing their final categories.
K–2.udl: Provide picture labels, tactile objects, and sentence frames. Offer simplified sets for emerging learners.
K–2.materials: Classroom objects, sorting mats, labels.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on sorting.
2. Model grouping objects.
3. Students sort objects.
4. Students create labeled charts.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide pre-made categories for support. Challenge advanced learners to sort by two attributes.
K–2.reflection: What rule did you use to sort your objects?

K–2.title: Binary Questions: Yes or No?
K–2.objective: Students will understand binary as a system of two choices by sorting objects using yes/no questions.
K–2.task: Students sort picture cards by answering a series of yes/no questions (e.g., 'Does it have wheels?'). They build a simple binary decision tree.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher listens as students justify their yes/no decisions.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students create a two-level yes/no decision tree using picture cards.
K–2.udl: Provide picture icons, verbal modeling, and sentence frames. Offer simplified sets for emerging learners.
K–2.materials: Picture cards, sorting mats, chart paper.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on binary choices.
2. Model a yes/no sort.
3. Students sort cards.
4. Students create decision trees.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide pre-made questions for support. Challenge advanced learners to create three-level trees.
K–2.reflection: How did your yes/no questions help you sort the objects?

K–2.title: Comic Strip Sequencing
K–2.objective: Students will arrange comic panels in logical order to understand sequencing and story flow.
K–2.task: Students receive mixed-up comic panels and work in pairs to arrange them into a logical story sequence.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher asks students to explain why one panel should come before another.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students create their own 3-panel comic showing a clear beginning, middle, and end.
K–2.udl: Provide visual panels, blank templates, and optional verbal retell. Offer simplified comics for emerging readers.
K–2.materials: Comic panels, blank templates, crayons.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on sequencing.
2. Model arranging comic panels.
3. Students sequence mixed-up comics.
4. Students create their own comics.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide fewer panels for support. Challenge advanced learners with 4–5 panel comics.
K–2.reflection: What clue helped you figure out the correct order?

K–2.title: Measure and Compare: Collecting Height Data
K–2.objective: Students will collect simple measurement data and compare results using a bar-style representation.
K–2.task: Students measure classroom objects using nonstandard units (e.g., cubes) and record the data. They then create a simple bar representation.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks whether students measure consistently and record data accurately.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students create a final bar-style chart comparing at least three objects.
K–2.udl: Provide visual rulers, tactile cubes, and sentence frames. Offer simplified measurement tasks for emerging learners.
K–2.materials: Cubes, objects to measure, chart paper.
K–2.vocabulary: measure, compare, data, chart, taller, shorter
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on measurement.
2. Model measuring with cubes.
3. Students measure objects.
4. Students create charts.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide pre-measured data for support. Challenge advanced learners to order objects from shortest to longest.
K–2.reflection: What did you notice when comparing your measurements?

K–2.title: Fix the Story: Debugging Mixed-Up Steps
K–2.objective: Students will identify and correct errors in a mixed-up set of story steps, building early debugging skills.
K–2.task: Students receive a scrambled set of steps for a simple task (e.g., making a sandwich). They identify the errors and rearrange the steps.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher asks students to explain why a step is incorrect or out of order.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students create a corrected step-by-step chart and illustrate one step.
K–2.udl: Provide picture steps, tactile cards, and sentence frames. Offer simplified tasks for emerging learners.
K–2.materials: Step cards, chart paper, crayons.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on debugging.
2. Model fixing a mixed-up task.
3. Students debug story steps.
4. Students create corrected charts.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide fewer steps for support. Challenge advanced learners with distractor steps.
K–2.reflection: How did you know which step was wrong?

K–2.title: Coding with Shapes: Create a Pattern Artwork
K–2.objective: Students will create a repeating pattern artwork using shape commands, understanding that art can be generated through algorithms.
K–2.task: Students use either unplugged shape cards or a simple drawing app to create a repeating pattern artwork following a shape-based algorithm.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks whether students can identify the repeating part of their pattern.
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students create a final artwork and label the repeating unit.
K–2.udl: Provide shape cards, digital drawing tools, and visual examples. Offer simplified patterns for emerging learners.
K–2.materials: Shape cards, drawing app, paper, crayons.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on shape patterns.
2. Model creating a pattern.
3. Students build patterns.
4. Students create final artworks.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide pre-made patterns for support. Challenge advanced learners to create two repeating units.
K–2.reflection: What shapes did you choose for your pattern and why?

K–2.title: If It’s Raining: Everyday Conditionals
K–2.objective: Students will understand conditionals by applying if/then logic to everyday weather-related decisions.
K–2.task: Students receive weather cards (rainy, sunny, snowy) and choose matching action cards (use umbrella, wear sunglasses). They match them using if/then statements.
K–2.formativeAssessment: Teacher asks students to verbalize their reasoning using the phrase 'If it is __, then I __.'
K–2.summativeAssessment: Students create a mini-book with three if/then weather rules and illustrate each one.
K–2.udl: Provide picture cards, sentence frames, and optional verbal explanations. Offer simplified choices for emerging learners.
K–2.materials: Weather cards, action cards, mini-book templates.
K–2.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on conditionals.
2. Model matching weather to actions.
3. Students match cards.
4. Students create mini-books.
5. Reflection.
K–2.differentiation: Provide pre-matched examples for support. Challenge advanced learners with if/then/else choices.
K–2.reflection: How did the weather change your decision? What rule did you use?

3–5.title: Recipe Algorithms: Writing Clear Instructions
3–5.objective: Students will write clear, precise multi-step algorithms for simple recipes and test them with peers.
3–5.task: Students choose a simple recipe (e.g., making trail mix) and write a step-by-step algorithm. Partners follow the algorithm exactly to test clarity.
3–5.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks whether steps are specific, ordered, and testable.
3–5.summativeAssessment: Students revise and submit a final algorithm with improvements based on peer testing.
3–5.udl: Provide visual recipe examples, sentence starters, and optional verbal dictation.
3–5.materials: Recipe samples, paper, pencils, optional snack materials.
3–5.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on precise instructions.
2. Model writing a recipe algorithm.
3. Students draft algorithms.
4. Peer testing.
5. Revision and reflection.
3–5.differentiation: Provide step starters for support. Challenge advanced learners to include conditional steps.
3–5.reflection: Which step needed the most revision and why?

3–5.title: Debug the Story Code
3–5.objective: Students will identify and correct errors in story-based pseudocode.
3–5.task: Students receive short story algorithms with intentional errors (missing steps, wrong order). They work in groups to debug and rewrite the corrected version.
3–5.formativeAssessment: Teacher listens for reasoning as students identify errors.
3–5.summativeAssessment: Students submit a corrected algorithm with annotations explaining each fix.
3–5.udl: Provide color-coded error clues, sentence frames, and optional verbal explanations.
3–5.procedures:
1. Mini-lesson on debugging.
2. Model debugging a short story.
3. Students debug story code.
4. Groups share fixes.
5. Reflection.
3–5.differentiation: Provide simpler stories for support. Challenge advanced learners with multiple errors.
3–5.reflection: How did you decide which part of the code was wrong?

3–5.title: Survey & Graph: Class Data Investigation
3–5.objective: Students will collect, organize, and represent data using bar graphs and draw conclusions.
3–5.task: Students design a survey question, collect class responses, and create a bar graph to represent the data.
3–5.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks whether students record data accurately and choose appropriate graph scales.
3–5.summativeAssessment: Students write two conclusions based on their graph.
3–5.udl: Provide graph templates, digital graphing tools, and sentence frames.
3–5.procedures:
